title: On Robust Prefix-Tuning for Text Classification

structured.summary: This paper proposes a novel robust prefix-tuning method that preserves the modularity and efficiency of prefix-tuning by tuning an extra prefix on-the-fly during inference to steer activations toward canonical manifolds constructed from clean training data. The approach improves robustness against diverse adversarial text attacks on multiple classification benchmarks without modifying the pretrained language model or incurring expensive adversarial training.

strengths.point: Preserves modularity and efficiency of prefix-tuning while improving robustness
strengths.evidence: The method adds an extra prefix tuned at inference time without modifying the pretrained LM or requiring full adversarial training, maintaining low storage and computational overhead.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical validation across multiple datasets and strong adversarial attacks
strengths.evidence: Evaluations on SST-2, AG’s News, and SNLI with five strong attacks show consistent robustness improvements over standard prefix-tuning, adversarial prefix-tuning, and data augmentation baselines.

strengths.point: Novel use of canonical manifolds to characterize correct model behavior
strengths.evidence: The method constructs low-dimensional PCA manifolds from activations of correctly classified training samples to guide extra prefix tuning during inference.

strengths.point: Insightful qualitative analysis of attention patterns under attack
strengths.evidence: Robust prefix-tuning leads to averaging attention over tokens or ignoring adversarial triggers, mitigating their effect.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to robustness methods for other parameter-efficient tuning approaches
weaknesses.evidence: No discussion or experiments comparing to robust adapters or LoRA-based defenses limits understanding of novelty within broader parameter-efficient tuning robustness literature.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient theoretical or mechanistic justification for tuning extra prefix only on bottom layers
weaknesses.evidence: The paper provides only empirical evidence without analysis or visualization explaining why bottom layers yield better robustness.

weaknesses.point: No analysis of sensitivity of canonical manifolds to noisy or mislabeled training data
weaknesses.evidence: The method assumes clean labels for manifold construction but does not evaluate robustness under label noise or domain shift.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation lacks stronger or more diverse robustness baselines and metrics
weaknesses.evidence: Baselines exclude certified defenses or detection-based methods; robustness metrics focus only on accuracy without calibration or robustness curves.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility hindered by missing critical hyperparameters and algorithmic details
weaknesses.evidence: Key inference tuning hyperparameters and pseudocode for manifold construction and projection are not provided.

weaknesses.point: Clarity issues in optimal control interpretation and attention visualization
weaknesses.evidence: The control theory framing is abstract without formal definitions; attention pattern changes lack quantitative metrics or clear visualization protocols.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on scalability and practical deployment constraints
weaknesses.evidence: No analysis of scaling to larger models or longer prefixes; batch-level extra prefix tuning may not suit single-instance or low-latency inference.

weaknesses.point: No quantitative analysis of computational overhead during inference
weaknesses.evidence: Claims of negligible overhead are not supported by runtime or memory benchmarks.

questions.question: How does the method perform under domain shift or with noisy/mislabeled training data?
questions.motivation: Robustness to distributional changes and label noise is critical for real-world deployment but remains untested.

questions.question: Can the approach be adapted for single-instance or low-latency inference scenarios?
questions.motivation: Many applications require fast, per-example inference, but the method currently tunes extra prefix per batch, which may limit practical use.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to robustness methods for other parameter-efficient tuning approaches. No discussion or experiments comparing to robust adapters or LoRA-based defenses limits understanding of novelty within broader parameter-efficient tuning robustness literature.
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient theoretical or mechanistic justification for tuning extra prefix only on bottom layers. The paper provides only empirical evidence without analysis or visualization explaining why bottom layers yield better robustness.
critique_points.point_003: No analysis of sensitivity of canonical manifolds to noisy or mislabeled training data. The method assumes clean labels for manifold construction but does not evaluate robustness under label noise or domain shift.
critique_points.point_004: Evaluation lacks stronger or more diverse robustness baselines and metrics. Baselines exclude certified defenses or detection-based methods; robustness metrics focus only on accuracy without calibration or robustness curves.
critique_points.point_005: Reproducibility hindered by missing critical hyperparameters and algorithmic details. Key inference tuning hyperparameters and pseudocode for manifold construction and projection are not provided.
critique_points.point_006: Clarity issues in optimal control interpretation and attention visualization. The control theory framing is abstract without formal definitions; attention pattern changes lack quantitative metrics or clear visualization protocols.
critique_points.point_007: Limited discussion on scalability and practical deployment constraints. No analysis of scaling to larger models or longer prefixes; batch-level extra prefix tuning may not suit single-instance or low-latency inference.
critique_points.point_008: No quantitative analysis of computational overhead during inference. Claims of negligible overhead are not supported by runtime or memory benchmarks.

transcript.reader:
**Structured Summary**

---

### Problem & Motivation

- **Context:** Large-scale pretrained language models (LMs) have achieved great success in NLP tasks. However, fine-tuning these models for downstream tasks is resource-intensive due to the need to update and store all model parameters.
- **Prefix-tuning:** A lightweight alternative that only optimizes continuous prefix embeddings prepended to each Transformer layer, requiring orders of magnitude fewer parameters than full fine-tuning.
- **Robustness Challenge:** Despite efficiency and modularity, prefix-tuning is vulnerable to adversarial text attacks (character-level, word-level, sentence-level, universal adversarial triggers).
- **Limitations of Existing Defenses:** Most textual defense methods require modifying the LM or maintaining auxiliary detectors, which compromises prefix-tuning’s modularity and low storage benefits. Adversarial training is also computationally expensive.
- **Research Question:** Can robustness of prefix-tuning be improved without sacrificing its efficiency, modularity, or modifying the pretrained LM?

---

### Proposed Method

- **Robust Prefix-Tuning Framework:** 
  - Maintains the original prefix embedding fixed after training.
  - During inference, adds an *extra batch-level prefix* tuned on-the-fly for each test batch to enhance robustness.
- **Key Idea:**
  - Collect layerwise activations at the output prediction position from correctly classified training samples.
  - Use PCA to project these activations onto low-dimensional *canonical manifolds* representing “correct” model behavior per layer.
  - For each test batch, tune the extra prefix to minimize the distance of the batch’s activations to these canonical manifolds, thus steering the LM towards correct predictions despite adversarial perturbations.
- **Implementation Details:**
  - Use GPT2-medium as the base LM.
  - Prefix length set to 10.
  - Tune extra prefix on bottom N=3 layers for best robustness.
  - The extra prefix is optimized with a few gradient steps during inference.
- **Interpretation:** The method can be viewed as a closed-loop optimal control problem, where the extra prefix acts as a control input to correct deviations caused by adversarial perturbations.

---

### Results

- **Datasets:** SST-2 (binary sentiment), AG’s News (topic classification), SNLI (natural language inference).
- **Attacks Evaluated:** Five strong textual attacks including PWWS, VIPER, SCPN, TextBugger (BUG), and Universal Adversarial Triggers (UAT).
- **Baselines:** Standard prefix-tuning, adversarial prefix-tuning (adversarial training on prefix embeddings), adversarial data augmentation.
- **Findings:**
  - The proposed robust prefix-tuning substantially improves robustness against all five attack types compared to all baselines.
  - It maintains comparable accuracy on clean (non-adversarial) inputs.
  - Robust prefix-tuning applied on adversarially trained prefixes yields the strongest robustness.
  - Adversarial training baselines require significantly longer training time and are less practical.
  - Adversarial data augmentation improves robustness mainly against the specific attack used for augmentation but can overfit and degrade robustness against other attacks.
  - Robust prefix-tuning consistently improves robustness across all attacks even when combined with adversarial data augmentation.
- **Layer Tuning Effect:**
  - Tuning bottom layers (N=3) yields better robustness improvements than tuning top layers or all layers.
- **Qualitative Analysis:**
  - For in-sentence attacks, robust prefix-tuning steers the LM to *average attention* over tokens, reducing focus on perturbed tokens.
  - For UAT attacks, it *ignores distracting adversarial triggers* by assigning them low attention weights, focusing on essential tokens.
- **Efficiency:**
  - The extra prefix tuning during inference adds negligible overhead compared to expensive adversarial training or data augmentation during training.

---

### Claimed Contributions

1. **Novel Robust Prefix-Tuning Framework:** First defense method for prefix-tuning that preserves its lightweight, modular nature without modifying the pretrained LM.
2. **Canonical Manifold Construction:** Introduces the idea of characterizing correct model behavior via low-dimensional manifolds of layerwise activations from clean data.
3. **On-the-fly Batch-Level Prefix Tuning:** Proposes tuning an additional prefix during inference to steer activations back to canonical manifolds for robustness.
4. **Extensive Empirical Validation:** Demonstrates substantial robustness improvements across multiple datasets and diverse adversarial attacks while maintaining clean accuracy.
5. **Interpretation via Optimal Control Theory:** Provides a theoretical perspective framing prefix-tuning and robust prefix-tuning as optimal and closed-loop control problems, respectively.
6. **Insights into Attention Mechanisms:** Shows how robust prefix-tuning modifies attention patterns to mitigate adversarial effects.
7. **Preservation of Efficiency and Modularity:** Achieves robustness without expensive adversarial training or architectural changes, maintaining practical deployment benefits.

---

**In summary,** this work addresses the robustness vulnerability of prefix-tuning in text classification by introducing a novel, efficient, and modular defense that leverages canonical activation manifolds and on-the-fly extra prefix tuning, validated by strong empirical results and grounded in optimal control theory.
transcript.critic_1:
1. **Novelty - Missing Comparison to Related Robustness Methods for Prefix-Tuning:**  
While the paper claims to be the first defense for prefix-tuning that preserves modularity and efficiency, it does not sufficiently discuss or compare to recent concurrent works that explore robustness in parameter-efficient tuning methods (e.g., adapters or LoRA). Including such comparisons or at least a discussion would clarify the novelty and positioning of the proposed method.

2. **Methodology - Justification for Selecting Bottom N=3 Layers for Extra Prefix Tuning:**  
The choice to tune the extra prefix only on the bottom three layers is empirically motivated (Section "Layer Tuning Effect"), but the paper lacks a theoretical or intuitive explanation why bottom layers are more effective for robustness. An ablation or analysis on why top or middle layers underperform would strengthen this design choice.

3. **Methodology - Assumption of Correctly Classified Training Samples for Canonical Manifold Construction:**  
The canonical manifolds are constructed from activations of correctly classified training samples, but the paper does not discuss how sensitive the method is to misclassifications or noisy labels in training data. This assumption may limit applicability in noisy or low-resource settings.

4. **Evaluation - Baselines Lack Stronger Robustness Methods Beyond Adversarial Training and Data Augmentation:**  
The evaluation compares against standard prefix-tuning, adversarial prefix-tuning, and adversarial data augmentation only. It omits other recent robustness techniques such as certified defenses, robust fine-tuning methods, or detection-based defenses that might be adapted to prefix-tuning. This limits understanding of how the method stacks up against the broader robustness literature.

5. **Evaluation - Metrics Focus Solely on Accuracy, Missing Other Robustness Measures:**  
The results report accuracy on clean and adversarial inputs but do not include other robustness metrics such as robustness curves over attack strength, confidence calibration, or detection rates of adversarial inputs. Including these would provide a more comprehensive robustness evaluation.

6. **Evaluation - Universal Adversarial Trigger (UAT) Attack Setup Details Are Sparse:**  
The paper evaluates on UAT but does not provide sufficient details on how the triggers are generated, their length, or whether the triggers are optimized per dataset or shared. This makes it difficult to assess the fairness and difficulty of the UAT evaluation.

7. **Reproducibility - Missing Hyperparameter Details for Extra Prefix Tuning at Inference:**  
The paper states that the extra prefix is optimized with a few gradient steps during inference but does not specify the learning rate, number of steps, batch size, or optimizer used. These details are critical for reproducing the on-the-fly tuning procedure.

8. **Reproducibility - Lack of Code or Pseudocode for Canonical Manifold Construction and Projection:**  
The method relies on PCA projections of layerwise activations, but the paper does not provide pseudocode or detailed algorithmic steps for constructing and applying these canonical manifolds, hindering reproducibility.

9. **Clarity - Explanation of the Optimal Control Interpretation Is Too Abstract:**  
The framing of robust prefix-tuning as a closed-loop optimal control problem is interesting but presented at a high level without concrete mathematical formulation or examples. This section could confuse readers unfamiliar with control theory and would benefit from clearer exposition.

10. **Clarity - Ambiguity in How Attention Patterns Are Quantitatively Measured:**  
The qualitative analysis claims that robust prefix-tuning leads to average attention over tokens or ignores adversarial triggers, but it is unclear how attention weights are quantitatively measured or visualized. More explicit metrics or visualization methods would improve clarity.

11. **Limitations - No Discussion on Computational Overhead During Inference:**  
Although the paper claims negligible overhead for extra prefix tuning at inference, it does not provide quantitative runtime or latency measurements. This omission leaves open questions about practical deployment feasibility, especially in real-time or resource-constrained environments.

12. **Limitations - Method Focused Only on Text Classification Tasks:**  
All experiments are conducted on classification datasets (SST-2, AG’s News, SNLI), but the paper does not discuss whether the approach generalizes to other NLP tasks such as generation, question answering, or structured prediction, limiting understanding of broader applicability.

13. **Generalisability - Unclear How Method Scales to Larger Models or Longer Prefixes:**  
The experiments use GPT2-medium and prefix length 10, but the paper does not explore or discuss how the canonical manifold approach and extra prefix tuning scale with larger models (e.g., GPT-3 scale) or longer prefixes, which may affect robustness and efficiency.

14. **Generalisability - Batch-Level Extra Prefix Tuning May Not Work for Single-Instance Inference:**  
The method tunes an extra prefix per test batch, but many real-world applications require single-instance inference. The paper does not address whether the approach can be adapted for single examples or how batch size affects robustness gains.

15. **Methodology - Potential Overfitting to Training Distribution in Canonical Manifolds:**  
Since canonical manifolds are constructed from training data activations, the method may be sensitive to domain shifts or distributional changes at test time. The paper does not analyze robustness under domain shift or out-of-distribution adversarial attacks.
transcript.auditor:
The Critic’s review is generally thorough and well-structured, but several points can be sharpened for specificity, better supported by evidence, and expanded to cover missed critical issues. Below is a detailed audit of each critique point, followed by a list of important issues the Critic missed.

---

### Detailed Audit of Critique Points

1. **Novelty - Missing Comparison to Related Robustness Methods for Prefix-Tuning**

- **Specificity:** The point is somewhat generic; it mentions “recent concurrent works” on adapters or LoRA but does not cite or name any specific papers or methods. It would be stronger if it identified concrete related works or at least categories of methods (e.g., adapter-based robustness, LoRA robustness) and specified what aspects are missing in comparison.
- **Evidence:** The paper’s summary does not mention any comparison to other parameter-efficient tuning robustness methods beyond prefix-tuning adversarial training and data augmentation, so this critique is supported.
- **Improvement:** The critique should push for explicit discussion or experimental comparison to at least one recent adapter or LoRA robustness method, or a clear justification why such comparisons are not applicable.

2. **Methodology - Justification for Selecting Bottom N=3 Layers for Extra Prefix Tuning**

- **Specificity:** The critique correctly notes the lack of theoretical or intuitive explanation beyond empirical results. It could be more specific by requesting, for example, an analysis of layerwise sensitivity to adversarial perturbations or ablation studies showing performance trends per layer.
- **Evidence:** The summary states only empirical motivation for bottom layers tuning, so this is valid.
- **Improvement:** The critique should explicitly ask for a mechanistic explanation or visualization (e.g., layerwise activation variance under attack) to justify this design choice.

3. **Methodology - Assumption of Correctly Classified Training Samples for Canonical Manifold Construction**

- **Specificity:** This is a well-targeted and specific critique. It could be improved by requesting experiments or analysis on noisy or mislabeled training data to assess robustness of manifold construction.
- **Evidence:** The paper relies on clean training activations; no mention of noise robustness is given.
- **Improvement:** The critique could push for sensitivity analysis or robustness checks under label noise or low-resource regimes.

4. **Evaluation - Baselines Lack Stronger Robustness Methods Beyond Adversarial Training and Data Augmentation**

- **Specificity:** The critique is somewhat generic; it mentions “certified defenses, robust fine-tuning methods, or detection-based defenses” but does not specify which ones might be applicable or feasible for prefix-tuning.
- **Evidence:** The summary lists only adversarial training and data augmentation baselines.
- **Improvement:** The critique should specify which recent robustness methods (e.g., randomized smoothing, certified robustness for text, or detection methods adapted to prefix-tuning) could be compared or discussed, or explain why they are not included.

5. **Evaluation - Metrics Focus Solely on Accuracy, Missing Other Robustness Measures**

- **Specificity:** This is a strong and specific point. The critique could be more precise by suggesting concrete metrics such as robustness curves over perturbation budgets, confidence calibration (ECE), or false positive rates for adversarial detection.
- **Evidence:** The summary only mentions accuracy metrics.
- **Improvement:** The critique should explicitly request these additional metrics and argue how they would provide a more nuanced robustness evaluation.

6. **Evaluation - Universal Adversarial Trigger (UAT) Attack Setup Details Are Sparse**

- **Specificity:** This is a precise and valid critique. It could be improved by requesting exact trigger length, trigger generation method, whether triggers are dataset-specific or universal, and how triggers are applied during evaluation.
- **Evidence:** The summary notes lack of details on UAT setup.
- **Improvement:** The critique should demand these details to assess reproducibility and fairness.

7. **Reproducibility - Missing Hyperparameter Details for Extra Prefix Tuning at Inference**

- **Specificity:** This is a concrete and important point. The critique could be more explicit by requesting learning rate, optimizer type, number of gradient steps, batch size, initialization of extra prefix, and stopping criteria.
- **Evidence:** The summary states only “a few gradient steps” without details.
- **Improvement:** The critique should emphasize that these details are critical for reproducing the inference-time tuning procedure.

8. **Reproducibility - Lack of Code or Pseudocode for Canonical Manifold Construction and Projection**

- **Specificity:** This is a strong and specific critique. It could be enhanced by requesting algorithmic steps or pseudocode for (1) collecting activations, (2) applying PCA, (3) projecting test activations, and (4) computing manifold distances.
- **Evidence:** The summary mentions PCA but no algorithmic details.
- **Improvement:** The critique should stress that without these details, reproducing the method is difficult.

9. **Clarity - Explanation of the Optimal Control Interpretation Is Too Abstract**

- **Specificity:** This is a well-targeted point. It could be improved by requesting concrete mathematical formulations, e.g., defining the control variables, system dynamics, cost functions, and how the extra prefix tuning corresponds to control inputs.
- **Evidence:** The summary notes the interpretation is high-level.
- **Improvement:** The critique should ask for illustrative examples or equations to clarify this novel framing.

10. **Clarity - Ambiguity in How Attention Patterns Are Quantitatively Measured**

- **Specificity:** This is a precise critique. It could be strengthened by requesting specific metrics (e.g., average attention entropy, attention weight distributions over tokens, or visualization methods) and statistical tests.
- **Evidence:** The summary mentions qualitative claims without quantitative backing.
- **Improvement:** The critique should push for quantitative analysis or clear visualization protocols.

11. **Limitations - No Discussion on Computational Overhead During Inference**

- **Specificity:** This is a concrete and important point. It could be improved by requesting actual runtime measurements, latency overhead, or memory consumption during extra prefix tuning.
- **Evidence:** The summary claims negligible overhead but provides no numbers.
- **Improvement:** The critique should demand empirical evidence to support efficiency claims.

12. **Limitations - Method Focused Only on Text Classification Tasks**

- **Specificity:** This is a valid and specific point. It could be enhanced by requesting discussion or preliminary experiments on other NLP tasks such as generation, QA, or structured prediction.
- **Evidence:** The summary only reports classification datasets.
- **Improvement:** The critique should ask for discussion on generalizability beyond classification.

13. **Generalisability - Unclear How Method Scales to Larger Models or Longer Prefixes**

- **Specificity:** This is a well-targeted point. It could be improved by requesting experiments or analysis on larger LMs (e.g., GPT-3 scale) or longer prefix lengths, and their impact on manifold construction and tuning efficiency.
- **Evidence:** The summary only uses GPT2-medium and prefix length 10.
- **Improvement:** The critique should ask for scalability analysis.

14. **Generalisability - Batch-Level Extra Prefix Tuning May Not Work for Single-Instance Inference**

- **Specificity:** This is a precise and important point. It could be strengthened
transcript.critic_2:
1. **Novelty - Missing Comparison to Robustness Methods for Parameter-Efficient Tuning Beyond Prefix-Tuning**  
The paper claims to be the first defense preserving modularity and efficiency for prefix-tuning robustness but omits comparison or discussion of recent robustness methods developed for other parameter-efficient tuning approaches such as adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019) or LoRA (Hu et al., 2021). For example, recent works on robust adapters or LoRA variants (e.g., adapter-based adversarial training or robust fine-tuning) are not cited or experimentally compared. Including at least a discussion or empirical comparison would clarify the novelty and situate the method within the broader parameter-efficient robustness literature (Section “Related Work” and “Experiments”).

2. **Methodology - Lack of Theoretical or Intuitive Justification for Tuning Extra Prefix Only on Bottom N=3 Layers**  
While Section “Layer Tuning Effect” empirically shows tuning the extra prefix on the bottom three layers yields better robustness, the paper does not provide a theoretical explanation or analysis of why bottom layers are more effective than middle or top layers. An ablation study analyzing layerwise sensitivity to adversarial perturbations or visualization of activation shifts per layer would strengthen this design choice and provide mechanistic insight.

3. **Methodology - Sensitivity of Canonical Manifold Construction to Noisy or Mislabeled Training Data Not Addressed**  
The canonical manifolds are constructed from activations of correctly classified training samples, assuming clean labels and data. The paper does not discuss or evaluate how noise in training labels or misclassifications affect the quality of these manifolds and downstream robustness. This assumption may limit applicability in noisy or low-resource settings. A sensitivity analysis or robustness check under label noise would be valuable (Section “Proposed Method”).

4. **Evaluation - Baselines Lack Stronger or More Diverse Robustness Methods Beyond Adversarial Training and Data Augmentation**  
The evaluation compares only to standard prefix-tuning, adversarial prefix-tuning, and adversarial data augmentation baselines. It omits recent robustness techniques such as certified defenses (e.g., randomized smoothing for text), robust fine-tuning methods, or detection-based defenses that could be adapted to prefix-tuning. Including or at least discussing these methods would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the method’s relative robustness (Section “Experiments”).

5. **Evaluation - Robustness Metrics Limited to Accuracy; Missing Other Important Measures**  
The reported results focus solely on accuracy under clean and adversarial inputs. The paper does not include robustness curves over varying attack strengths, confidence calibration metrics (e.g., Expected Calibration Error), or adversarial detection rates. These additional metrics would provide a more nuanced and comprehensive evaluation of robustness and model reliability (Section “Results”).

6. **Evaluation - Insufficient Details on Universal Adversarial Trigger (UAT) Attack Setup**  
The paper evaluates robustness against UAT attacks but provides sparse details on trigger generation: trigger length, whether triggers are dataset-specific or universal, optimization procedure, and how triggers are applied during evaluation. Without these details, it is difficult to assess the fairness, difficulty, and reproducibility of the UAT evaluation (Section “Results”).

7. **Reproducibility - Missing Critical Hyperparameters for Extra Prefix Tuning During Inference**  
The method involves on-the-fly tuning of an extra prefix per test batch with “a few gradient steps,” but the paper does not specify key hyperparameters such as learning rate, optimizer type, number of gradient steps, batch size, initialization of the extra prefix, or stopping criteria. These details are essential for reproducing the inference-time tuning procedure (Section “Proposed Method”).

8. **Reproducibility - Lack of Algorithmic Details or Pseudocode for Canonical Manifold Construction and Projection**  
The canonical manifolds are constructed via PCA on layerwise activations, but the paper does not provide pseudocode or detailed algorithmic steps for (1) collecting activations, (2) applying PCA, (3) projecting test activations onto manifolds, or (4) computing manifold distances used for extra prefix tuning. This hinders reproducibility and clarity of the method (Section “Proposed Method”).

9. **Clarity - Optimal Control Interpretation Presented Too Abstractly Without Concrete Formulation**  
The framing of robust prefix-tuning as a closed-loop optimal control problem is interesting but described only at a high level without formal mathematical definitions of system dynamics, control variables, cost functions, or explicit examples. This may confuse readers unfamiliar with control theory. Including concrete equations or illustrative examples would clarify this novel perspective (Section “Proposed Method”).

10. **Clarity - Ambiguity in Quantitative Measurement and Visualization of Attention Pattern Changes**  
The qualitative analysis claims that robust prefix-tuning leads to averaging attention over tokens or ignoring adversarial triggers, but it is unclear how attention weights are quantitatively measured or visualized. The paper lacks explicit metrics (e.g., attention entropy, token-level attention distributions) or visualization protocols. Providing quantitative analyses or clear visualization methods would improve clarity and support claims (Section “Qualitative Analysis”).

11. **Limitations - No Quantitative Analysis of Computational Overhead During Inference**  
Although the paper claims that extra prefix tuning during inference adds negligible overhead compared to adversarial training, it does not provide runtime measurements, latency benchmarks, or memory consumption data. This omission leaves open questions about practical deployment feasibility, especially in real-time or resource-constrained environments (Section “Efficiency”).

12. **Limitations - Focus Restricted to Text Classification Tasks Without Discussion of Broader Applicability**  
All experiments are conducted on classification datasets (SST-2, AG’s News, SNLI), but the paper does not discuss whether the approach generalizes to other NLP tasks such as text generation, question answering, or structured prediction. This limits understanding of the method’s broader applicability and potential limitations (Section “Experiments”).

13. **Generalisability - Unclear How Method Scales to Larger Models or Longer Prefix Lengths**  
The experiments use GPT2-medium and prefix length 10, but the paper does not explore or discuss how the canonical manifold approach and extra prefix tuning scale with larger models (e.g., GPT-3 scale) or longer prefixes. Larger models and longer prefixes may affect robustness, computational cost, and manifold construction quality. A scalability analysis or discussion is needed (Section “Experiments”).

14. **Generalisability - Batch-Level Extra Prefix Tuning May Not Be Feasible for Single-Instance or Low-Latency Inference**  
The method tunes an extra prefix per test batch, but many real-world applications require single-instance inference or have strict latency constraints. The paper does not address whether the approach can be adapted for single examples or how batch size affects robustness gains and computational cost. This limits practical applicability (Section “Proposed Method”).

15. **Methodology - Potential Overfitting to Training Distribution in Canonical Manifolds and Lack of Domain Shift Analysis**  
Since canonical manifolds are constructed from training data activations, the method may be sensitive to domain shifts or distributional changes at test time. The paper does not analyze robustness under domain shift or out-of-distribution adversarial attacks, which is critical for real-world deployment (Section “Proposed Method” and “Results”).
